ignore-generated-files.label: Ignore Generated Files
ignore-generated-files.summary: Write narrow ignore rules, prove which rule matches, and separate “ignored” from “already tracked.”

sketch0.title: Keep Rebuildable Output Out of History

try_ignore_generated_files_sketch0.title: Add an Ignore Rule for Build Output
try_ignore_generated_files_sketch0.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `release-kit/` repository.

`dist/app.js` is generated output. Create `.gitignore` in the repository root and add one line: `dist/`.

Then open Terminal, run `git status --short`, and confirm that the generated file disappears while `.gitignore` remains visible.
try_ignore_generated_files_sketch0.hint: Start in the editor and make only the content change described in the task.

help.concept: .gitignore rules keep matching untracked generated files out of normal status and staging workflows.
help.hint_1: Keep the file path and exact requested text in view.
help.hint_2: Switch to the terminal only after the saved file matches the scenario.

try_ignore_generated_files_sketch0.instructions:
You are working in the prepared `release-kit/` repository.

`dist/app.js` is generated output. Create `.gitignore` in the repository root and add one line: `dist/`.

Then open Terminal, run `git status --short`, and confirm that the generated file disappears while `.gitignore` remains visible.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf release-kit
mkdir -p release-kit
git -C release-kit init -q -b main
git -C release-kit config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C release-kit config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd release-kit
printf %s '# Release Kit
' > README.md
cd ..
git -C release-kit add -A
git -C release-kit commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd release-kit
mkdir -p dist
printf %s 'bundled output
' > dist/app.js
cd ..

git -C release-kit add -A
git -C release-kit commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd release-kit
mkdir -p dist
printf %s 'bundled output
' > dist/app.js
cd ..

try_ignore_generated_files_sketch1.title: Explain Why a File Is Ignored
try_ignore_generated_files_sketch1.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `release-kit/` repository.

The repository already ignores `logs/`. Use `git check-ignore -v` to identify the rule that matches `logs/error.log`.
try_ignore_generated_files_sketch1.hint: Ask Git to explain whether the generated path matches an ignore rule.

help.concept: git check-ignore -v reports the matching rule and file, which makes ignore behavior explainable instead of mysterious.
help.hint_1: Use the exact generated path from the scenario.
help.hint_2: The result should identify the intended policy rather than a broader accidental rule.

try_ignore_generated_files_sketch1.instructions:
You are working in the prepared `release-kit/` repository.

The repository already ignores `logs/`. Use `git check-ignore -v` to identify the rule that matches `logs/error.log`.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf release-kit

git -C release-kit config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd release-kit
printf %s '# Release Kit
' > README.md
cd ..
cd release-kit
printf %s 'logs/
' > .gitignore
cd ..
git -C release-kit add -A
git -C release-kit commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd release-kit
mkdir -p logs
printf %s 'preview warning
' > logs/error.log
cd ..

try_ignore_generated_files_sketch2.title: Stop Tracking a Local Configuration File
try_ignore_generated_files_sketch2.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `release-kit/` repository.

`config/local.json` was committed by mistake. The ignore rule now exists. Remove the file from Git’s index without deleting the working copy, then check status.
try_ignore_generated_files_sketch2.hint: Remove the path from Git’s index while keeping the local working copy.

help.concept: An ignore rule does not automatically untrack a file; git rm --cached removes it from the index while preserving the working copy.
help.hint_1: The ignore rule should already exist before you stop tracking the file.
help.hint_2: Verify that the repository records a removal while the local file remains.

try_ignore_generated_files_sketch2.instructions:
You are working in the prepared `release-kit/` repository.

`config/local.json` was committed by mistake. The ignore rule now exists. Remove the file from Git’s index without deleting the working copy, then check status.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf release-kit
mkdir -p release-kit/config
git -C release-kit init -q -b main
git -C release-kit config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C release-kit config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
printf '%s
' '{"port":3000}' > release-kit/config/local.json
git -C release-kit add config/local.json
git -C release-kit commit -q -m "Add local configuration"
printf '%s
' 'config/local.json' > release-kit/.gitignore
git -C release-kit add .gitignore
git -C release-kit commit -q -m "Ignore local configuration"

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf release-kit
mkdir -p release-kit/config
git -C release-kit init -q -b main
git -C release-kit config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C release-kit config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
printf '%s
' '{"port":3000}' > release-kit/config/local.json
git -C release-kit add config/local.json
git -C release-kit commit -q -m "Add local configuration"
printf '%s
' 'config/local.json' > release-kit/.gitignore
git -C release-kit add .gitignore
git -C release-kit commit -q -m "Ignore local configuration"

try_ignore_generated_files_sketch3.title: Commit the Ignore Policy
try_ignore_generated_files_sketch3.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `release-kit/` repository.

`.gitignore` is staged with rules for `dist/` and `*.log`. Commit it as `Ignore generated output`, then verify that build and log files remain ignored.
try_ignore_generated_files_sketch3.hint: Record only the staged snapshot and use the message requested by the scenario.

help.concept: Committing .gitignore shares the project policy while ignored generated files remain outside history.
help.hint_1: The message should describe the project result, not the commands you used.
help.hint_2: After recording it, inspect history and any work that remains.

try_ignore_generated_files_sketch3.solutionCode:
git commit -m "Ignore generated output"
git check-ignore dist/app.js debug.log
git status --short
try_ignore_generated_files_sketch3.instructions:
You are working in the prepared `release-kit/` repository.

`.gitignore` is staged with rules for `dist/` and `*.log`. Commit it as `Ignore generated output`, then verify that build and log files remain ignored.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf release-kit
mkdir -p release-kit
git -C release-kit init -q -b main
git -C release-kit config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C release-kit config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd release-kit
printf %s '# Release Kit
' > README.md
cd ..
git -C release-kit add -A
git -C release-kit commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd release-kit
printf %s 'dist/
*.log
' > .gitignore
cd ..
cd release-kit
mkdir -p dist
printf %s 'bundled output
' > dist/app.js
cd ..
cd release-kit
printf %s 'local log
' > debug.log
cd ..
git -C release-kit add -- .gitignore

printf %s '# Release Kit
' > README.md
cd ..
git -C release-kit add -A
git -C release-kit commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd release-kit
printf %s 'dist/
*.log
' > .gitignore
cd ..
cd release-kit
mkdir -p dist
printf %s 'bundled output
' > dist/app.js
cd ..
cd release-kit
printf %s 'local log
' > debug.log
cd ..
git -C release-kit add -- .gitignore

ignore-generated-files-single-choice-0.title: Purpose of .gitignore
ignore-generated-files-single-choice-0.prompt: What is the primary purpose of a `.gitignore` file in a Git repository?
ignore-generated-files-single-choice-0.hint: Focus on what the command or concept observes or changes at this point in the workflow.

help.concept: Write narrow ignore rules, prove which rule matches, and separate “ignored” from “already tracked.”
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.a: To track changes in all files
options.b: To ignore specific files and directories
options.c: To delete files from the repository
options.d: To rename files in the repository

ignore-generated-files-single-choice-1.title: Effect of Adding to .gitignore
ignore-generated-files-single-choice-1.prompt: What happens when you add a file to `.gitignore` that has already been committed?
ignore-generated-files-single-choice-1.hint: Focus on what the command or concept observes or changes at this point in the workflow.

help.concept: Write narrow ignore rules, prove which rule matches, and separate “ignored” from “already tracked.”
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.a: The file is automatically deleted from the repository
options.b: The file is untracked and removed from history
options.c: The file remains tracked and in history
options.d: The file is renamed in the repository

ignore-generated-files-multi-choice-0.prompt: Which of the following patterns are commonly used in a `.gitignore` file? Select all that apply.
ignore-generated-files-multi-choice-0.hint: Judge each option independently; choose only the statements that stay true in this exact repository state.

help.concept: Write narrow ignore rules, prove which rule matches, and separate “ignored” from “already tracked.”
help.hint_1: A partly true option is still wrong if it adds an effect Git does not guarantee.
help.hint_2: Use the layer named in the prompt—working tree, staging area, or history—to test each choice.

ignore-generated-files-drag-reorder-0.prompt: Arrange the steps to ignore a file that has already been committed.
ignore-generated-files-drag-reorder-0.hint: Start with the state that must exist before the next action can make sense.

ignore-generated-files-sketch0.title: Keep Rebuildable Output Out of History
ignore-generated-files-sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
Build artifacts, previews, caches, and logs can usually be recreated. Recording them adds noise, larger commits, and avoidable conflicts without preserving meaningful source work.

A `.gitignore` rule describes paths Git should leave untracked. A directory rule such as `dist/` is clearer and safer than an overly broad file-type rule when only one generated directory should be excluded.

Commit the policy file itself so every contributor shares the same boundary.

Suppose a build creates `build/app.js`. Add `build/` as a line in the repository’s `.gitignore`, then inspect the state:

```bash
git status --short
```

The generated file should disappear from the untracked list while `.gitignore` itself remains visible as meaningful project configuration.

ignore-generated-files-sketch1.title: Prove Which Rule Matches
ignore-generated-files-sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
Do not assume an ignore rule works merely because a file disappeared from status. Ask Git to explain the match. The verbose ignore check identifies both the path and the rule responsible.

That evidence is especially useful when several ignore files or patterns overlap. A silent result means the path did not match an active rule.

Verification turns `.gitignore` from guesswork into an inspectable policy.

Ask Git which rule ignores a path:

```bash
git check-ignore -v cache/output.tmp
```

The verbose result names the ignore file, the matching rule, and the path. That is stronger evidence than simply noticing that status is quiet.

ignore-generated-files-sketch2.title: Ignoring Is Not the Same as Untracking
ignore-generated-files-sketch2.bodyMarkdown:
Ignore rules affect paths Git is not already tracking. If `config/local.json` was committed earlier, adding a rule does not remove it from the index or from future snapshots.

To correct the boundary, stop tracking the path while keeping the local working copy. The next commit records the repository-level removal, and the ignore rule prevents the local file from returning as untracked work.

Review this carefully: the file stays on disk, but it leaves the project’s tracked state.

When a local settings file was already committed, remove only its index entry:

```bash
git rm --cached settings/local.json
git status --short
```

`--cached` keeps the working copy on disk while staging its removal from future snapshots. The ignore rule can then keep it untracked.

ignore-generated-files-sketch3.title: Prefer the Narrowest Useful Rule
ignore-generated-files-sketch3.bodyMarkdown:
An ignore rule can hide too much. `dist/` protects one generated directory; `*.html` might hide source pages the project needs.

Use status to see the repository-level effect and an ignore check to see the rule-level explanation. Together they prove that the policy excludes the intended output and nothing broader.

A production-ready ignore file is small, specific, and tied to real generated artifacts.

Record the ignore policy, then verify representative generated paths:

```bash
git commit -m "Ignore build output"
git check-ignore build/app.js debug.log
git status --short
```

The commit preserves the policy, `check-ignore` proves the paths match, and status reveals whether any meaningful configuration change remains.
